text.title: gemini-basics Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for gemini-basics. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: For new projects, default to Gemini 2.0 Flash unless explicit requirements force a different choice. Use Gemini 1.5 Pro only for complex reasoning or contexts >500K tokens. Use Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking for math, logic puzzles, and complex multi-step reasoning.
rule.rationale: Flash is 10x cheaper than Pro and fast enough for most agent pipeline steps. The cost difference justifies the minor quality trade-off for bounded tasks. Pro is necessary only for long-context analysis or open-ended reasoning. Thinking models expose reasoning steps explicitly, beneficial for debugging complex logic but not needed for simple tasks.

rule.statement: Install the official google-generativeai SDK via pip. For authentication, use environment variable GOOGLE_API_KEY when working with Google AI Studio (free tier or development). For production, use Google Cloud and Vertex AI with gcloud application-default login (no API key in code).
rule.rationale: Environment variables prevent credentials from being checked into git. For production, Vertex AI with workload identity is the secure path; it avoids API keys entirely.

rule.statement: Always set max_output_tokens in generation_config. Omitting it allows the model to generate arbitrarily long responses, multiplying cost unexpectedly. Choose 1024–8192 depending on task.
rule.rationale: Output token count directly determines cost. Unlimited output has caused budget blowouts in production; it is a common oversight with high consequence.

rule.statement: Use model.start_chat() to initialize a session. Persist the chat object across turns; do not create a new chat for each message. The history is automatically maintained by the model.
rule.rationale: Chat history enables context-aware follow-ups. Creating new chats for each turn loses all prior context, forcing repetition in prompts and wasting tokens.

rule.statement: Always check response.prompt_feedback.block_reason before accessing response.text. If the prompt is blocked, response.candidates will be empty and indexing response.candidates[0] will raise IndexError. Wrap text access in a helper that returns None on block, then log the block reason for audit.
rule.rationale: Safety blocks occur on the prompt, not the output; blocked responses have no candidates. Accessing .text on a blocked response raises ValueError. Logging block reasons helps identify if a class of inputs is systematically blocked.

rule.statement: Use Gemini 2.0 Flash by default for cost optimization; it is 10-40x cheaper than Pro depending on context size. Enable context caching only when the same large prompt prefix (system prompt + document) appears in >5 requests per session; below that, the overhead is not worth it. Set max_output_tokens explicitly to avoid runaway cost.
rule.rationale: Flash pricing is ~$0.10 per 1M input tokens; Pro is $1.25+. Context caching saves 75% on cached tokens but only amortizes if the same prefix is reused many times. Most sessions are one-off questions; caching helps only in high-volume document analysis scenarios.

rule.statement: Use gemini-2.0-flash as the default for agent pipeline steps; only escalate to Pro when context >128K tokens or quality is clearly insufficient for the task.
rule.rationale: Flash is fast and cheap; reserving it for 80% of tasks keeps costs down while maintaining quality for the 20% that need Pro's reasoning.

rule.statement: Do not mix -latest model aliases (e.g., gemini-1.5-pro-latest) in production. Pin specific model versions (e.g., gemini-1.5-pro-002) so version upgrades are intentional and tested.
rule.rationale: -latest aliases silently upgrade the model on Google's schedule; production pipelines should control upgrade timing and test before rolling out.

rule.statement: Persist chat.history across turns; validate the last response before appending to history to prevent inconsistency after blocked responses.
rule.rationale: Chat history is mutable; if you append to it after a blocked response, the history becomes inconsistent and subsequent messages lose context.

rule.statement: Store API keys in environment variables or managed vaults (1Password, Google Cloud Secret Manager); never check them into git. For Vertex AI in production, use workload identity federation instead of service account JSON files.
rule.rationale: Credentials in git are compromised immediately; service account files are difficult to rotate. Workload identity is the secure production pattern.

rule.statement: A subagent setting up Gemini basics should configure API key from env, select the model based on task complexity (Flash for speed, Pro for long context, Thinking for math), set generation_config with explicit max_output_tokens and temperature, and wrap the call with safety check + retry logic.
rule.rationale: This workflow ensures credentials are secure, cost is bounded, safety is checked, and transient errors are retried.
